title: Will Latvia seek shelter under France’s “nuclear umbrella”?
content:
At the beginning of the year, as the United States intensified its ambition to take control of Greenland, European leaders actively debated whether Europe itself needs its own “nuclear umbrella”. At the Munich Security Conference, Latvia’s Prime Minister Evika Siliņa (“New Unity”) told Politico: “Why not?”

Now, France — the only nuclear-armed country in the European Union — has announced a new nuclear doctrine and listed the countries with which it will conduct joint nuclear military exercises. Latvia is not on France’s list. Why? “The choice of specific countries for joint military exercises with France is decided by France itself. That does not mean that opportunities for cooperation with other countries do not exist,” Siliņa explained to Delfi.

According to Politico, on the 2nd of March, French President Emmanuel Macron delivered an important speech in which he announced that France would increase the number of nuclear weapons at its disposal and would no longer publicly disclose their exact number. “One of our submarines carries as much striking power as all the bombs dropped in Europ

creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Romania Isn’t Handing Black Sea Gas to Ukraine
content:
After Romania and Ukraine signed a framework agreement on cooperation in the energy sector, senator Petrișor Peiu (AUR, a far-right party) claimed that Romgaz will store in Ukraine the gas from future production in the Neptun Deep field, and that it will be “consumed” by the neighboring state. The document says something entirely different.

Under the signature of Nicușor Dan and “to the applause of a coalition hysterically obsessed with the idea of destroying any Romanian sovereignty,” the authorities in Bucharest have concluded “the most shameful” document in history, after the friendship treaty with the former USSR signed by the Iliescu regime on April 5, 1991, claimed AUR senator Petrișor Peiu, referring to the Fr
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Chinese company Alibaba is planning a data centre in Finland – debate over risks continues
content:
According to the Finnish Security and Intelligence Service (Supo), authoritarian states can use data centres to acquire Western technology and utilise it as part of soft influence. Alongside TikTok, Alibaba Cloud is now also preparing a data centre project in Finland.

At least two data centre projects with links to major Chinese companies are under development in Finland. According to Faktabaari’s investigative work, the Chinese digital giant Alibaba Cloud is set to enter the Finnish data centre market.

Having established a subsidiary in Finland in February 2026, Alibaba Cloud is using LinkedIn job advertisements to recruit, for example,
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Poland turns North: The Baltic Region’s Push for Collective Security
content:
A decade ago, the Baltic Sea region occupied a secondary place in European foreign policy. Today, it has returned to centre stage. Following the accession of Finland and Sweden to NATO, Poland, together with the Nordic countries, has a chance to shape a new regional security architecture.

The Baltic has become a key area for the security of the entire European continent. Russia’s aggression against Ukraine, NATO’s enlargement, and the growing importance of protecting energy and digital infrastructure have turned the region into a strategic priority – both militarily and economically.

Recognising these challenges, NATO launched Operation Baltic Sentry in January 2025, aimed at increasing naval presence as well as monitoring and protecting un
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: “Make Europe Great Again”: Orbán’s Vision of Reshaping the EU
content:
Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán delivered a speech at a gathering of conservative and nationalist leaders in Budapest, declaring that the West is undergoing “the greatest political transformation in Western civilization in a hundred years.”

In his remarks, he praised Donald Trump, criticized Brussels, and predicted victory in Hungary’s upcoming parliamentary elections.

Budapest hosted the CPAC (Conservative Political Action Conference), one of the largest and most influential gatherings of conservative politicians, activists, and thinkers who describe themselves as “patriots” from Europe and the Americas.

“Within a year or two, the entire Western world will shine in patriotic, national, and conservative colors — exactly as
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Europe Under Hybrid Fire: Russia Targets NATO’s Eastern Flank
content:
The Russian Federation has for years been waging a multidimensional hybrid war against Europe, combining cyberattacks, disinformation operations, espionage, physical sabotage, and the instrumentalisation of migration. Poland is not merely a target, but a testing ground where Moscow probes the limits of the entire North Atlantic Alliance’s resolve.

Hybrid threats – activities conducted below the threshold of war, combining cyberattacks, information operations, intelligence activities, and sabotage—have become one of the key tools of competition in the 21st century. Their effectiveness stems not only from the difficulty of clearly identifying the perpetrator, but also from their ability to exert gradual pressure on societies, economies, and state insti
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Whip for Big Tech or Threat to Free Speech? Czechia Faces DSA Backlash
content:
Czechia is grappling with mounting political tensions over legislation meant to apply the EU’s Digital Services Act (DSA), a landmark regulation targeting online platforms. While some politicians and lobby groups warn of censorship, the European Commission has taken legal action against Prague for failing to adopt the law.

Most Czechs agree that the government should actively intervene to combat the spread of false and manipulative information. This is according to a survey conducted last year by the Central European Digital Media Observatory (CEDMO). Overall, 65% of respondents supported government interv
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Democracy Decided by Algorithm: How Romania Became the First Country to Cancel Elections Because of TikTok
content:
Romania is still trying to understand how it set a precedent that many analysts consider dangerous. The cancellation of the 2024 presidential elections, due to manipulation through TikTok, marked the defeat of democracy in the face of social networks, a Romanian researcher argues in a study published in March.

Credited in polls with only a few percentage points, Călin Georgescu came first in the first round of the presidential elections on November 24, 2024. A public figure previously considered marginal, Georgescu quickly transformed into a candidate supported by an overwhelming wave of messages on social media, which portrayed him almost as a messianic figure on TikTok, Facebook, YouTube, and other platforms.

Behind this
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: Equal Opportunities in Europe? A Growing Skepticism Among the Young
content:
Talent, hard work or family wealth? What really determines success today? Young Europeans grew up believing they lived on a continent of equal opportunity. Today, they increasingly feel that success depends not on skills or effort, but on their parents’ financial resources.

Europe is often portrayed as a continent of social fairness – a place where prosperity, universal public education, and integration projects were meant to level the playing field. Programs such as Erasmus+, free or low-cost higher education in many countries, and extensive welfare systems were all designed with this goal in mind.

In theory, these mechanisms were supposed to ensure that a young European – regardless of where they were born—would have a similar start
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: What the war in Iran means for Ukraine and Russia
content:
A war with Iran could influence the course of the conflict in Ukraine. Rising oil prices, limited weapons supplies, and shifting Western priorities may simultaneously strengthen Russia while complicating Kyiv’s position.

At first glance, a conflict between Iran and the United States and their allies in the Middle East should have little impact on the war that has been ongoing in Ukraine since 2022. The two are separated by thousands of kilometers, shaped by different strategic realities and driven by distinct political objectives. However, in an era of global military, energy, and financial interdependence, any major conflict acts like a tectonic shock – shifting the balance of power even in regions far removed from the battlefield.

For bot
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: More renovation and construction, less bureaucracy – that’s how the EU will tackle the housing crisis
content:
Unspent funds from the Recovery plan could be redirected toward affordable housing. However, there are concerns that lowering standards will only benefit the industry.

Mobilizing funds and other financial resources to boost housing construction and the renovation of aging and energy-inefficient buildings, along with relaxing construction regulations – these are the main measures outlined by the European Parliament after more than a year of analysis and discussion by the Special Committee on the Housing Crisis (HOUS). It tackles an issue that has become a problem for millions of Europeans: housing is scarce and unaffordable. Ultimately, it has proposed a solution, though one that has so far been met with mixed reactions.
creator: Olga Jurkowska

title: A desperate struggle for power: how Orban uses hatred towards Ukraine in his election campaign
content:
Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán, facing the threat of defeat in the parliamentary elections scheduled for April, cannot stop talking about Ukraine and its president, Volodymyr Zelenskyy. These conclusions were drawn in early March by the media outlet Kyiv Independent, and it is difficult to disagree with them.

As recently as early February, Viktor Orbán described the war-torn country as Hungary’s “enemy.” On the 7th of February, speaking at an election rally, the prime minister criticised Ukraine for its demand that the European Union (EU) halt imports of cheap Russian energy: “Anyone who says such a thing is Hungary’s enemy; therefore, Ukraine is our enemy.”

These comments came shortly after the EU Council approved plans to completely ban Russian gas imports by the end of 2027. Complaints were voiced not only by Hungary but also by Slovakia, which is similarly dependent on Moscow’s resources, with Budapest even taking the matter to the EU court.

The issue of Russian energy supplies is further complicated by the conflict in the Middle East, which has triggered the highest rise in energy pri
